items.q: Does Big-O notation tell you how many seconds an algorithm will take?
items.correct: No - Big-O describes the growth rate of work relative to input size, ignoring constants and hardware; actual seconds depend on the machine and implementation.
items.misconception: Yes - O(n) means the algorithm takes n seconds to run.
items.random_wrong: Yes - Big-O is measured with a stopwatch.
items.why: The abstraction is the point and the stumbling block. Big-O compares scalability of algorithms, letting us reason independently of any particular computer.

items.q: Is quicksort always faster than merge sort?
items.correct: No - quicksort averages O(n log n) but degrades to O(n^2) with poor pivots, while merge sort guarantees O(n log n) in every case.
items.misconception: Yes - the name quicksort means it is the fastest sort in all cases.
items.random_wrong: Yes - merge sort is only used when quicksort is unavailable.
items.why: The name oversells it. Pivot choice matters: already-sorted input with a naive pivot is quicksort's worst case, something exam questions love to probe.

items.q: Can A* use any heuristic and still guarantee the shortest path?
items.correct: No - the heuristic must be admissible, never overestimating the true remaining cost; an overestimating heuristic can make A* return a suboptimal path.
items.misconception: Yes - any heuristic works because A* checks every route anyway.
items.random_wrong: Yes - heuristics only change the colour of the path found.
items.why: The heuristic looks like a free speed-up. Optimality is conditional: admissibility keeps the goal's true cost from being masked by inflated estimates.

items.correct: How resource use grows as the input size grows
items.misconception: The exact running time of a program in seconds
items.random_wrong: The number of lines of code in an algorithm
items.why: It's How resource use grows as the input size grows. “The exact running time of a program in seconds” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Partitioning the list around a pivot and recursively sorting each partition
items.misconception: Repeatedly swapping the first and last items
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Partitioning the list around a pivot and recursively sorting each partition. “Repeatedly swapping the first and last items” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A* differs from Dijkstra's algorithm because A*:
items.correct: Adds a heuristic estimate of the distance remaining to the goal
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Adds a heuristic estimate of the distance remaining to the goal. “Only works on unweighted graphs” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The worst-case time complexity of linear search on a list of n items is:
items.why: Correct answer: O(n). “O(1)” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many comparisons does one full pass of bubble sort make on a list of 6 items?
items.why: Exact answer: 5 comparisons. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
